1: A shadow: 'vindicate me, O LORD, for I have walked in my integrity, and I have trusted in the LORD without wavering.' The vindication prayer — 'vindicate me' (shophteni, judge me, give me justice). The resurrection is the divine vindication of Jesus: 'declared to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead' (Romans 1:4). The 'walking in integrity without wavering' is fulfilled only in Jesus — whose trust in the Father never wavered even through the cry of dereliction.
2: A shadow: 'prove me, O LORD, and try me; test my heart and my mind.' The invitation to divine examination — utterly confident only in the mouth of the sinless one. Jesus was tested supremely in Gethsemane and on the cross; the Father's examination found no wavering. The testing of Psalms 26:2 is what Jesus passed perfectly — 'he learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8) and emerged from every test without sin (Hebrews 4:15: 'one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin').
3: A theme: 'for your steadfast love is before my eyes, and I walk in your faithfulness.' Walking in the light of divine chesed — the chesed-shaped life. Christ's life was the supreme embodiment of walking in divine faithfulness: 'the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing' (John 5:19). The faithfulness of the Father before Jesus's eyes shaped every step of his ministry, the cross, and the resurrection.
4: A theme: 'I do not sit with men of falsehood, nor do I consort with hypocrites.' The moral separation — not associating with the deceitful. Jesus ate with sinners but never participated in their sin; he engaged the hypocrites (Pharisees, scribes) but never adopted their hypocrisy. His sharpest rebukes were for religious hypocrisy (Matt 23). The one who sat with sinners in order to save them never sat among the hypocritical in their hypocrisy.
5: A theme: 'I hate the assembly of evildoers, and I will not sit with the wicked.' The hatred of evil as a moral affirmation, not mere avoidance. Jesus's 'cleansing of the temple' (John 2:13-17; Matthew 21:12-13) is the active expression of this hatred: turning over the money-changers' tables, driving out those who had turned the house of prayer into a market. 'Zeal for your house will consume me' (John 2:17, quoting Psalms 69:9) — the hatred of what desecrates the holy.
6: A shadow: 'I wash my hands in innocence and go around your altar, O LORD.' 'I wash my hands in innocence' — the ritual declaration of purity before approaching the altar. The grim irony of the Passion narrative: Pilate 'washed his hands before the crowd, saying, I am innocent of this man's blood' (Matthew 27:24) — performing the ritual of Psalms 26:6 over the one who actually had clean hands. The innocent one was handed over by the one who washed his hands to claim innocence.
7: A theme: 'proclaiming thanksgiving aloud, and telling all your wondrous deeds.' The proclamation of YHWH's wondrous works — the todah testimony. Christ's own proclamation of the Father's works: 'I have manifested your name to the people whom you gave me' (John 17:6). After the resurrection, the proclamation of 'wondrous deeds' becomes the content of apostolic preaching: the miracle of the cross and resurrection as YHWH's supreme niphlaot.
8: A revelation of God: 'O LORD, I love the habitation of your house and the place where your glory dwells.' The love of the divine dwelling — the temple as the place of divine presence. Jesus identifies himself as the new temple: 'destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up... he was speaking about the temple of his body' (John 2:19-21). The love of the place where glory dwells is the love of Christ himself — in whom 'the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Colossians 2:9).
9: A theme: 'do not sweep my soul away with sinners, nor my life with bloodthirsty men.' The petition for separation from the fate of sinners. Christ was 'numbered with the transgressors' (Isaiah 53:12; Mark 15:27-28: crucified between two criminals) voluntarily — not swept away with them but willingly placed among them to bear their fate. The distinction of Psalms 26:9 was preserved in his innocence even while he bore the punishment of the guilty.
10: A theme: 'in whose hands are evil devices, and whose right hands are full of bribes.' The characterization of the wicked — evil hands and bribed right hands. Judas received thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:15) — the bribe that handed over the innocent. The evil devices and bribes of Psalms 26:10 are fulfilled in the betrayal that set the Passion in motion.
11: A shadow: 'but as for me, I shall walk in my integrity; redeem me, and be gracious to me.' The integrity-walk as the ground of the redemption-petition. Christ walked in absolute integrity and was redeemed from death by the Father's grace — the resurrection as the divine graciousness extended to the one who walked perfectly. Those who are 'in Christ' walk in his imputed integrity and receive the same redemption (Ephesians 1:7: 'in him we have redemption through his blood').
12: A theme: 'my foot stands on level ground; in the great assembly I will bless the LORD.' The stable standing and public praise — the posture of the vindicated worshiper. Christ's resurrection appearances are his 'standing on level ground' before the community: he stands, eats, shows his wounds, and commissions his disciples. The 'great assembly' where blessing occurs is the church — his ongoing body through whom he continues to bless the Father.
